xEnrich 0.1.0
  • Get started
  • Reference
  • Changelog

xEnrich: from gene list to pathway, organ, and cell type

Source: vignettes/xEnrich.Rmd
xEnrich.Rmd

The problem

Standard enrichment analysis returns dozens of nominally significant pathways, but doesn’t distinguish independent biological signals from redundant re-descriptions of the same gene set. It also can’t tell you where in the body a pathway is active.

xEnrich addresses both gaps:

  • Track A identifies which pathways are altered and separates independent signals from redundant shadows using conditional mutual information.
  • Track B identifies where in the body the gene list is most relevant and drills down to cell types.

This vignette demonstrates the full workflow on synthetic data.

Setup: synthetic data with known ground truth 

set.seed(42)
n_samples <- 120
n_genes   <- 500
genes     <- paste0("G", seq_len(n_genes))

# Two independent latent factors drive two clusters of genes
factor_A <- rnorm(n_samples)
factor_B <- rnorm(n_samples)

expr <- matrix(
  rnorm(n_samples * n_genes),
  nrow     = n_samples,
  dimnames = list(paste0("S", seq_len(n_samples)), genes)
)

# Inject signal into two gene blocks
expr[, 1:40]    <- expr[, 1:40]    + 2.5 * factor_A
expr[, 300:340] <- expr[, 300:340] + 2.0 * factor_B

# Phenotype: weighted sum of both factors + noise
phenotype <- 0.7 * factor_A + 0.5 * factor_B + rnorm(n_samples, sd = 0.4)

# Pathway definitions: two true signals, one shadow, one noise
gene_sets <- list(
  immune_response   = genes[1:40],           # true signal (factor A)
  immune_regulation = genes[1:35],           # redundant shadow of immune_response
  solute_transport  = genes[300:340],        # true signal (factor B)
  cell_adhesion     = genes[200:230],        # noise
  lipid_metabolism  = genes[400:430]         # noise
)

Track A: pathway-phenotype association

Step 1: run_info_assoc()

Mutual information between each pathway’s mean activity and the phenotype, calibrated by permutation:

assoc <- run_info_assoc(
  expr      = expr,
  phenotype = phenotype,
  gene_sets = gene_sets,
  n_perm    = 500,
  seed      = 42
)
#> Auto-selected nbins = 4 (Rice rule, capped for joint-table density and 20).

assoc[, c("set", "set_size", "MI_bits", "z_score", "p_value", "padj")]
#>                 set set_size    MI_bits    z_score     p_value       padj
#> 1   immune_response       40 0.49279195 23.6340430 0.001996008 0.00499002
#> 2 immune_regulation       35 0.49279195 23.6340430 0.001996008 0.00499002
#> 3  solute_transport       41 0.05631933  2.3759702 0.029940120 0.04990020
#> 4     cell_adhesion       31 0.00000000 -0.6145792 1.000000000 1.00000000
#> 5  lipid_metabolism       31 0.00000000 -0.6135967 1.000000000 1.00000000

Three pathways are significant: immune_response, immune_regulation (its shadow), and solute_transport. The noise pathways are correctly non-significant.

Step 2: run_assoc_redundancy_selection()

Which of these are independent findings?

sel <- run_assoc_redundancy_selection(
  assoc_results = assoc,
  expr          = expr,
  phenotype     = phenotype,
  gene_sets     = gene_sets,
  alpha         = 0.05,
  seed          = 42
)
#> 3 / 5 pathways significant at padj <= 0.05.
#> Auto-selected nbins = 4.
#> Auto-selected min_gain = 0.07392 bits (noise floor = 0.06697, relevance = 0.07392 [15% of leading MI]).
#> Stopping at step 3: best CMI = 0.03088 bits < min_gain = 0.07392.

sel[, c("step", "pathway", "marginal_mi", "conditional_mi",
        "redundancy_ratio", "cumulative_bits")]
#>                  step          pathway marginal_mi conditional_mi
#> immune_response     1  immune_response  0.49279195      0.4927920
#> solute_transport    2 solute_transport  0.05631933      0.3800016
#>                  redundancy_ratio cumulative_bits
#> immune_response          1.000000       0.4927920
#> solute_transport         6.747269       0.8727936

The redundancy ratio reveals the answer:

  • immune_response and solute_transport have ratios near 1 — they are independent signals.
  • immune_regulation has a ratio near 0 — its association with the phenotype is entirely explained by immune_response.

Step 3: classify_assoc_selection()

Translate ratios into plain-language actions:

classified <- classify_assoc_selection(sel)

classified[, c("pathway", "marginal_strength", "signal_class", "action")]
#>                           pathway marginal_strength signal_class
#> immune_response   immune_response            strong      primary
#> solute_transport solute_transport              weak  independent
#>                                            action
#> immune_response                            report
#> solute_transport low confidence, treat cautiously

Step 4: summarize_selection()

Translate MI into variance explained — the table a clinician can read:

summary_sel <- summarize_selection(classified, expr, phenotype, gene_sets)

summary_sel[, c("pathway", "signal_class", "marginal_r2",
                "incremental_r2", "cumulative_r2")]
#>                           pathway signal_class marginal_r2 incremental_r2
#> immune_response   immune_response      primary  0.60712013      0.6071201
#> solute_transport solute_transport  independent  0.09628523      0.1738994
#>                  cumulative_r2
#> immune_response      0.6071201
#> solute_transport     0.7810195
cat(sprintf(
  "%d independent pathways explain %.1f%% of phenotype variance (adj R2 = %.1f%%)\n",
  nrow(summary_sel),
  attr(summary_sel, "total_r2") * 100,
  attr(summary_sel, "adj_r2") * 100
))
#> 2 independent pathways explain 78.1% of phenotype variance (adj R2 = 77.7%)
cat(sprintf("Residual unexplained: %.1f%%\n", attr(summary_sel, "residual_pct")))
#> Residual unexplained: 21.9%

Step 5: compute_pathway_activity()

Extract per-sample activity scores for the selected pathways, ready for heatmaps, mediation models, or external regression:

act <- compute_pathway_activity(
  expr      = expr,
  gene_sets = gene_sets[sel$pathway],
  score     = "mean_z"
)

cat("Activity matrix:", nrow(act), "samples x", ncol(act), "pathways\n")
#> Activity matrix: 120 samples x 2 pathways
cat("Correlation with phenotype:\n")
#> Correlation with phenotype:
round(cor(act, phenotype), 3)
#>                   [,1]
#> immune_response  0.779
#> solute_transport 0.310

Visualise: information gain per selection step 

plot_gains(sel)

Track A: gene-list enrichment

When you have only a gene list (no expression matrix), use the gene-list lane.

Enrichment + redundancy in gene space 

# Simulate a DE gene list
gene_list <- unique(c(
  sample(genes[1:40],    30),   # enriched in immune_response
  sample(genes[300:340], 20),   # enriched in solute_transport
  sample(genes[100:200], 5)     # a few noise genes
))

# MI-scored enrichment
enrich <- run_info_enrichment(
  gene_list = gene_list,
  gene_sets = gene_sets,
  universe  = genes,
  n_perm    = 500,
  seed      = 42
)

enrich[, c("set", "overlap", "set_size", "info_bits", "ratio",
           "padj_emp", "padj_enrich")]
#>                 set overlap set_size  info_bits    ratio   padj_emp
#> 1   immune_response      30       40 0.15335126 6.818182 0.00332668
#> 2 immune_regulation      25       35 0.11709572 6.493506 0.00332668
#> 3  solute_transport      20       41 0.05955720 4.434590 0.00332668
#> 5  lipid_metabolism       0       31 0.01078215 0.000000 0.03992016
#> 4     cell_adhesion       0       31 0.01078215 0.000000 0.04191617
#>    padj_enrich
#> 1 5.308314e-24
#> 2 7.209783e-19
#> 3 1.820082e-10
#> 5 1.000000e+00
#> 4 1.000000e+00

Note how immune_regulation is significant by hypergeometric p-value (padj_enrich) but has lower MI (info_bits) than immune_response because its genes are a subset — less informative.

# Redundancy selection in gene space
sel_gl <- run_redundancy_selection(
  gene_list      = gene_list,
  gene_sets      = gene_sets,
  universe       = genes,
  initial_scores = setNames(enrich$info_bits, enrich$set),
  seed           = 42
)
#> Auto-selected min_gain = 0.023 bits (noise floor = 0.01078, relevance threshold = 0.023 [15% of leading MI]).
#> Stopping at step 3: best conditional gain = 0.00215 bits < min_gain = 0.023.

sel_gl[, c("step", "pathway", "marginal_mi", "conditional_mi",
           "redundancy_ratio")]
#>                  step          pathway marginal_mi conditional_mi
#> immune_response     1  immune_response   0.1533513      0.1533513
#> solute_transport    2 solute_transport   0.0595572      0.1185829
#>                  redundancy_ratio
#> immune_response          1.000000
#> solute_transport         1.991077

Gene-space CMI correctly identifies immune_regulation as redundant with immune_response.

Track A: GSEA lane

When you have a full ranked list:

# Gene-level statistics: correlation with phenotype
gene_stats <- setNames(
  apply(expr, 2, function(g) cor(g, phenotype)),
  genes
)

batch <- run_batch_gsea(
  gene_sets  = gene_sets,
  gene_stats = gene_stats,
  n_perm     = 500,
  adaptive   = TRUE,
  seed       = 42
)
#> Running GSEA on 5 pathways [adaptive = TRUE, mode = 'refine', nthreads = 1].
#> Done. 3 / 5 pathways significant at padj <= 0.05.
#> Refine mode: 5/5 pathways resolved at 500 perms; 0 borderline pathways refined (0 extra perms).

batch$summary[, c("pathway", "enrichment_score", "p_value", "padj",
                   "leading_edge_size")]
#>             pathway enrichment_score     p_value       padj leading_edge_size
#> 1   immune_response        1.0000000 0.001996008 0.00332668                40
#> 2 immune_regulation        0.9892473 0.001996008 0.00332668                35
#> 3  solute_transport        0.8714597 0.001996008 0.00332668                41
#> 4     cell_adhesion       -0.3871642 1.000000000 1.00000000                12
#> 5  lipid_metabolism       -0.1812367 0.964071856 1.00000000                31
# Redundancy on leading edges
sel_gsea <- run_gsea_redundancy_selection(
  gsea_results = batch$results,
  gene_stats   = gene_stats,
  alpha        = 0.05,
  seed         = 42
)
#> 3 / 5 pathways significant at alpha = 0.05.
#> Leading-edge union: 81 genes (16.2% of universe).
#> Computing IT enrichment scores on leading edges...
#> Running CMI redundancy selection on leading edges...
#> Auto-selected min_gain = 0.03708 bits (noise floor = 0.00638, relevance threshold = 0.03708 [15% of leading MI]).
#> Stopping at step 3: best conditional gain = 0 bits < min_gain = 0.03708.

# Classify
classified_gsea <- classify_gsea_selection(sel_gsea)
classified_gsea[, c("pathway", "signal_class", "action")]
#>                           pathway signal_class              action
#> solute_transport solute_transport  independent report as secondary
#> immune_response   immune_response  independent report as secondary

Adaptive permutation p-values

xEnrich reports confidence intervals on permutation p-values. Here is one pathway in detail:

res <- run_enrichment(
  gene_list     = gene_sets$immune_response,
  gene_stats    = gene_stats,
  method        = "gsea",
  n_perm        = 500,
  adaptive      = TRUE,
  adaptive_mode = "decision",
  alpha         = 0.05,
  seed          = 42,
  keep_trace    = TRUE
)

inf <- res$inference$res
cat(sprintf(
  "ES = %.3f\np = %.4f\nCI = [%.4f, %.4f]\nPermutations used: %d\nDecision: %s\n",
  res$enrichment_score, inf$p,
  inf$p_ci_low, inf$p_ci_high,
  inf$B, inf$decision_alpha
))
#> ES = 1.000
#> p = 0.0066
#> CI = [0.0012, 0.0366]
#> Permutations used: 150
#> Decision: sig

The Wilson confidence interval makes the uncertainty in the p-value estimate explicit. Standard tools report only the point estimate.

Track B: location enrichment

Track B requires Human Protein Atlas and Tabula Sapiens reference data. These are downloaded once via load_reference() and cached locally:

# Not run — requires network access on first call
hpa_rna       <- load_reference("rna_hpa", source = "hpa")[[1]]
tabula_kidney <- load_reference("kidney",  source = "tabula")[[1]]

Tissue-level enrichment 

# Tissue enrichment using HPA RNA data
tissue_result <- enrich_by_hpa(
  gene_list      = my_gene_list,
  reference_df   = hpa_rna,
  group_col      = "Tissue",
  expression_col = "nTPM",
  method         = "hypergeometric",
  cutoff_type    = "quantile",
  cutoff_value   = 0.9,
  universe       = my_universe,
  universe_type  = "provided"
)

Full pipeline: organ → cell type 

# All-in-one: HPA tissue enrichment -> Tabula cell-type drill-down
pipeline <- run_location_pipeline(
  gene_list   = my_gene_list,
  hpa_data    = hpa_rna,
  tabula_data = list(kidney = tabula_kidney),
  hpa_type    = "rna",
  seed        = 42
)

print(pipeline)    # organ-level summary
summary(pipeline)  # flat data frame of top cell types

Convergence: WHAT + WHERE

The gene list bridges both tracks:

gene list → Track A: immune_response     → WHAT  (pathway)
         → Track B: cerebral cortex     → WHERE (organ)
                    → neurons           → WHERE (cell type)
         ↓
         Together: "immune response / cerebral cortex / neurons"

This convergence — naming the biology, the organ, and the cell type from a single gene list — is what xEnrich enables that no single existing tool provides.

Session info 

sessionInfo()
#> R version 4.5.3 (2026-03-11)
#> Platform: x86_64-pc-linux-gnu
#> Running under: Ubuntu 24.04.3 LTS
#> 
#> Matrix products: default
#> BLAS:   /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/openblas-pthread/libblas.so.3 
#> LAPACK: /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/openblas-pthread/libopenblasp-r0.3.26.so;  LAPACK version 3.12.0

#>  [1] LC_CTYPE=C.UTF-8       LC_NUMERIC=C           LC_TIME=C.UTF-8
